subject: [PATCH v3] memcg: cache obj_stock by memcg, not by objcg pointer

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary:
Author downplayed a concern about potential memory leaks due to bounded nature of the issue and previous behavior of silently dropping sub-page charges.

Author acknowledged that the patch may have issues for long-running cgroups, but decided that adding complexity to fix this issue is not worth it.
reviews.raw_body:
This is not much a concern as it is bounded (i.e. nr_nodes * PAGE_SIZE - 1) and
previously for the warning case, we were already dropping the sub-page charges.

---

This can be a concern for a long running cgroup. However fixing this would add
complexity not worth it. This is a temporary fix and will be reverted in newer
kernels.

2026-05-18.patch_summary: This patch fixes a performance regression in the Linux kernel's memory control group (memcg) implementation, specifically when dealing with cross-NUMA workloads. The issue arises from the fact that obj_stock_pcp caches are keyed by pointer to obj_cgroup instead of memcg, leading to unnecessary drains and misses on every refill and consume operation. To fix this, the patch changes the cache keying to use memcg instead of obj_cgroup pointer, allowing siblings to share the reserve without re-caching. This temporary fix will be reverted when per-node kmem accounting is introduced.

reviews.author: Qi Zheng
reviews.summary: Reviewer Qi Zheng acknowledged the patch as a temporary fix, expressed no further concerns or suggestions, and gave an Acked-by tag.
reviews.raw_body:
I think this is fine as a temporary fix:

Acked-by: Qi Zheng <qi.zheng@linux.dev>

Thanks!

2026-05-19.patch_summary: This patch fixes a performance regression in the Linux kernel's memory control group (memcg) implementation, specifically when dealing with cross-NUMA workloads. The issue arises from the fact that obj_stock_pcp caches are keyed by pointer to obj_cgroup instead of memcg, leading to unnecessary drains and misses on every refill and consume operation. To fix this, the patch changes the cache keying to use memcg instead of obj_cgroup pointer, allowing siblings to share the reserve without re-caching. This temporary fix will be reverted when per-node kmem accounting is introduced.
